title: The Memory Hierarchy — Speed vs Capacity
key_insight: Memory speed and capacity are always in tension. The faster the memory, the less of it you can afford.
body:
## The Memory Hierarchy

Every computer has multiple layers of memory, each trading speed for capacity:

```
Fastest / Smallest
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │  CPU Registers              │  ~1 cycle    ~KB
  │  L1 Cache                   │  ~4 cycles   ~64 KB
  │  L2 Cache                   │  ~12 cycles  ~256 KB
  │  L3 Cache (shared)          │  ~40 cycles  ~8–32 MB
  │  RAM                        │  ~100 cycles ~8–64 GB
  │  SSD                        │  ~100,000 cycles  ~TB
  │  HDD                        │  ~10,000,000 cycles ~TB
Slowest / Largest
```

### Cache: The Bridge Between Registers and RAM

The CPU is so fast it would starve waiting for RAM. Cache stores recently-used data much closer to the processor:

```python
# This loop is cache-friendly (sequential memory access)
for i in range(1000000):
    x = data[i]  # each access is adjacent in memory

# This loop is cache-unfriendly (random memory access)
import random
indices = list(range(1000000))
random.shuffle(indices)
for i in indices:
    x = data[i]  # each access jumps to random location — cache miss
```

Cache-unfriendly access can be 100× slower than cache-friendly access.

### GPU Memory (VRAM)

GPUs have their own RAM, called VRAM (Video RAM), separate from system RAM:
- Transfer between system RAM and VRAM is slow (PCIe bus)
- All GPU computation happens inside VRAM
- This is why you want to keep tensors on GPU — moving them back to CPU RAM is expensive

```python
import torch

# Fast: stays on GPU
result = tensor_a.cuda() + tensor_b.cuda()

# Slow: moves GPU tensor to CPU RAM
result_cpu = result.cpu().numpy()
```

### Memory-Mapped Files

For datasets too large for RAM, memory-mapped files let you access parts of a file as if it were RAM — the OS loads only what you access:

```python
import numpy as np
# 100 GB dataset — loads on demand, doesn't fill RAM
dataset = np.memmap('huge_dataset.npy', dtype='float32', mode='r', shape=(1000000, 10000))
sample = dataset[42]  # loads only this row from disk
```
common_mistakes: Moving tensors between GPU and CPU inside a training loop — do all computation on GPU, only move final results
Ignoring cache locality when writing custom data pipelines — sequential access is dramatically faster than random access
Assuming system RAM and VRAM are interchangeable — they require explicit transfers
mini_exercise: Run this in Python and time both versions: sequential vs random array access over 1M elements. Measure the difference using time.perf_counter(). You'll see cache effects directly.
